ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a clearly new application (the first parser for UCCA) and a novel transition set (including NODE and SWAP) designed to handle UCCA-specific phenomena: reentrancy (multiple parents), discontinuous units, and non-terminal nodes. While it builds on established techniques (transition-based parsing, neural classifiers, BiLSTMs), the specialized transition system and its applicability to semantic DAGs represent a non-trivial extension beyond prior work and are clearly differentiated from baselines. This would be informative to experts working on semantic DAG parsing and related representations. | B(4): The paper introduces the first parser specifically for UCCA and a novel transition set (including NODE and SWAP) that handles multiple parents (reentrancy), discontinuous units and non-terminal nodes, addressing formal properties of UCCA DAGs not covered by prior parsers. While it leverages known components (neural classifier, BiLSTM feature extractor), the transition-based, grammarless parsing approach and its transition set constitute a non-trivial and clearly differentiated extension beyond existing methods. This would inform experts working on semantic DAG parsing, though core learning components are standard.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a clearly new application (the first parser for UCCA) and a novel transition set (including NODE and SWAP) designed to handle UCCA-specific phenomena: reentrancy (multiple parents), discontinuous units, and non-terminal nodes. While it builds on established techniques (transition-based parsing, neural classifiers, BiLSTMs), the specialized transition system and its applicability to semantic DAGs represent a non-trivial extension beyond prior work and are clearly differentiated from baselines. This would be informative to experts working on semantic DAG parsing and related representations.

While it leverages known components (neural classifier, BiLSTM feature extractor), the transition-based, grammarless parsing approach and its transition set constitute a non-trivial and clearly differentiated extension beyond existing methods. This would inform experts working on semantic DAG parsing, though core learning components are standard.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper tackles an important gap by presenting TUPA, the first parser for UCCA that directly supports reentrancy, discontinuous units, and non-terminal nodes (full UCCA DAGs). Its neural BiLSTM-based parser outperforms a range of strong baselines both in-domain and out-of-domain, showing practical value, and the novel transition set (NODE, SWAP) is likely to inform development of parsers for other semantic DAG representations and languages with discontinuities. Some dataset/training limitations (sentence-level training, discarded linkage/implicit nodes, and conversion-induced upper bounds for baselines) slightly temper breadth of impact but do not undercut the contribution’s significance for semantic parsing research. | B(4): This paper addresses an important gap by presenting TUPA, the first parser for UCCA that handles multiple parents (reentrancy), discontinuous units and non-terminal nodes, i.e., the full formal properties of UCCA DAGs. Coupling the novel transition set with a neural classifier and BiLSTM feature extractor yields strong empirical gains—outperforming a range of strong baselines both in-domain and out-of-domain—suggesting the approach is practically useful. The transition set and grammarless, transition-based design are likely to inform development of parsers for other semantic DAG formalisms and languages with frequent discontinuities, opening significant new research directions and uptake within semantic parsing work.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper tackles an important gap by presenting TUPA, the first parser for UCCA that directly supports reentrancy, discontinuous units, and non-terminal nodes (full UCCA DAGs). Its neural BiLSTM-based parser outperforms a range of strong baselines both in-domain and out-of-domain, showing practical value, and the novel transition set (NODE, SWAP) is likely to inform development of parsers for other semantic DAG representations and languages with discontinuities. Some dataset/training limitations (sentence-level training, discarded linkage/implicit nodes, and conversion-induced upper bounds for baselines) slightly temper breadth of impact but do not undercut the contribution’s significance for semantic parsing research.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses an important gap by presenting TUPA, the first parser for UCCA that handles multiple parents (reentrancy), discontinuous units and non-terminal nodes, i.e., the full formal properties of UCCA DAGs. Coupling the novel transition set with a neural classifier and BiLSTM feature extractor yields strong empirical gains—outperforming a range of strong baselines both in-domain and out-of-domain—suggesting the approach is practically useful. The transition set and grammarless, transition-based design are likely to inform development of parsers for other semantic DAG formalisms and languages with frequent discontinuities, opening significant new research directions and uptake within semantic parsing work.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): Yes. The paper presents TUPA, a transition-based neural parser (with BiLSTM features) for UCCA semantic DAGs supporting reentrancy, discontinuities and non-terminals, which is squarely within top-tier NLP/ML conference topics (semantic parsing, parsing algorithms, neural models). It uses methods and evaluation standards expected at the venue—comparisons to strong baselines (DAGParser, TurboParser, Malt, LSTM parser), clear metrics (labeled precision/recall/F for primary and remote edges), and experiments on standard UCCA corpora (Wiki and 20K Leagues), with thorough related work coverage. Minor dataset limitations are acknowledged (sentence-level training, discarded inter-sentence/linkage/implicit nodes and conversion upper-bounds), but these do not affect fit for the venue. | B(5): The paper tackles semantic parsing by presenting TUPA, the first parser for UCCA, which is squarely within the scope of top-tier NLP/ML venues. It uses contemporary neural methods (BiLSTM feature extractor, DyNet implementation) and a novel transition set to handle UCCA's DAG properties, and evaluates against strong baselines (DAGParser, TurboParser, UPARSE, MaltParser, Stack-LSTM) on standard UCCA corpora (Wiki and 20K Leagues) with appropriate labeled precision/recall/F metrics. With thorough related work coverage, reported hyperparameters, and clear relevance to transition-based and semantic parsing research, the paper is an excellent fit for the venue.

reviewer_a.justification: Yes. The paper presents TUPA, a transition-based neural parser (with BiLSTM features) for UCCA semantic DAGs supporting reentrancy, discontinuities and non-terminals, which is squarely within top-tier NLP/ML conference topics (semantic parsing, parsing algorithms, neural models). It uses methods and evaluation standards expected at the venue—comparisons to strong baselines (DAGParser, TurboParser, Malt, LSTM parser), clear metrics (labeled precision/recall/F for primary and remote edges), and experiments on standard UCCA corpora (Wiki and 20K Leagues), with thorough related work coverage. Minor dataset limitations are acknowledged (sentence-level training, discarded inter-sentence/linkage/implicit nodes and conversion upper-bounds), but these do not affect fit for the venue.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles semantic parsing by presenting TUPA, the first parser for UCCA, which is squarely within the scope of top-tier NLP/ML venues. It uses contemporary neural methods (BiLSTM feature extractor, DyNet implementation) and a novel transition set to handle UCCA's DAG properties, and evaluates against strong baselines (DAGParser, TurboParser, UPARSE, MaltParser, Stack-LSTM) on standard UCCA corpora (Wiki and 20K Leagues) with appropriate labeled precision/recall/F metrics. With thorough related work coverage, reported hyperparameters, and clear relevance to transition-based and semantic parsing research, the paper is an excellent fit for the venue.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The experiments are fairly comprehensive: TUPA is evaluated in-domain and out-of-domain on two UCCA corpora, compared against multiple strong baselines, and reported with labeled precision/recall/F separately for primary and remote edges, which supports the main claims. However, the summary does not mention explicit ablation or sensitivity analyses of model components (e.g., removing BiLSTM or transition types), which is a notable gap. Related work coverage is described as thorough, and several limitations are discussed (sentence-level training, discarded linkage/implicit nodes, and conversion-induced upper bounds). Reproducibility is reasonably supported by reporting hyperparameters and implementation details (DyNet, dynamic oracle), but code availability is not mentioned, slightly reducing reproducibility confidence. | B(4): The experiments are broad and compare the proposed TUPA (with a neural BiLSTM classifier) against multiple strong baselines on two corpora (Wiki and 20K Leagues), reporting labeled precision/recall/F separately for primary and remote edges and evaluating both in-domain and out-of-domain performance, which supports the main claims. However, no ablation or sensitivity analyses are mentioned in the summary (e.g., the contribution of the transition set, NODE/SWAP, or BiLSTM features), and code release is not reported, which limits reproducibility despite reported hyperparameters and implementation details. The authors do acknowledge several limitations (sentence-level training, discarded inter-sentence/linkage/implicit nodes, and upper bounds from bilexical conversion), and related work coverage is described as thorough. Overall this is solid and mostly comprehensive but misses some important analyses and reproducibility conveniences.

However, no ablation or sensitivity analyses are mentioned in the summary (e.g., the contribution of the transition set, NODE/SWAP, or BiLSTM features), and code release is not reported, which limits reproducibility despite reported hyperparameters and implementation details. CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized and follows a clear intro -> method -> experiments -> conclusion flow, as evidenced by the detailed description of the TUPA transition-based parser and its experimental evaluation. The main contributions are stated early and explicitly (first UCCA parser, handling reentrancy/discontinuities, novel transitions such as NODE and SWAP). Notation and presentation appear consistent and well-defined (BiLSTM feature extractor, dynamic oracle, reported hyperparameters), and figures/tables are effective insofar as the work reports labeled precision/recall/F for primary and remote edges across multiple strong baselines and two datasets. Overall writing quality is good, so only minor presentation issues are suggested rather than major revisions. | B(4): The paper appears well-organized—introducing the problem and claims (TUPA and its capabilities) before detailing the transition-based method, neural implementation, and experiments on Wiki and 20K Leagues—so the flow from method to experiments to conclusion is logical. The main contributions are stated early (first parser for UCCA; support for reentrancy, discontinuities, non-terminals; neural/BiLSTM coupling) and related work coverage is thorough. Notation and formal properties are treated concretely (UCCA graphs as DAGs; explicit transition set including NODE and SWAP), hyperparameters and evaluation metrics/baselines are reported, and overall writing quality is described as good. Minor presentation gaps remain (e.g., code availability not mentioned and no explicit comment on the effectiveness of figures/tables in the summary), so I rate clarity as very good but not flawless.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The main claims are clearly supported by empirical evidence: a transition-based parser (TUPA) using a BiLSTM feature extractor is evaluated on two UCCA datasets (Wiki and 20K Leagues) and reported to outperform a range of strong baselines, and the transition set addresses reentrancy, discontinuities and non-terminals. The experimental methodology is largely sound — multiple competitive baselines (DAGParser, TurboParser, UPARSE, MaltParser, an LSTM parser), relevant labeled P/R/F metrics for primary and remote edges, dynamic-oracle training, and hyperparameters are reported. Minor technical concerns remain: conversion of gold graphs to bilexical graphs for some baselines discards non-terminal information (an acknowledged upper-bound limitation on comparisons), and no statistical significance testing is mentioned; these issues do not invalidate the main technical claims but temper the strength of comparative claims. | B(4): The main claims (a transition-based parser TUPA handling reentrancy, discontinuities and non-terminals, using a BiLSTM classifier, and outperforming a range of strong baselines in in- and out-of-domain experiments on the Wiki and 20K Leagues corpora) are supported by experimental results against reasonable baselines and appropriate labeled precision/recall/F measures. The paper’s methodology appears sound (use of strong baselines, BiLSTM feature extractor, dynamic oracle training, and reported hyperparameters), but there are methodological caveats: sentence-level training that discards inter-sentence/linkage/implicit nodes and the conversion of graphs to bilexical graphs/trees for baseline comparison (which the authors acknowledge creates an upper bound) could affect comparability and generality. Overall the technical work is solid and the conclusions are justified, with only minor issues that do not overturn the main claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The main claims are clearly supported by empirical evidence: a transition-based parser (TUPA) using a BiLSTM feature extractor is evaluated on two UCCA datasets (Wiki and 20K Leagues) and reported to outperform a range of strong baselines, and the transition set addresses reentrancy, discontinuities and non-terminals. The experimental methodology is largely sound — multiple competitive baselines (DAGParser, TurboParser, UPARSE, MaltParser, an LSTM parser), relevant labeled P/R/F metrics for primary and remote edges, dynamic-oracle training, and hyperparameters are reported. Minor technical concerns remain: conversion of gold graphs to bilexical graphs for some baselines discards non-terminal information (an acknowledged upper-bound limitation on comparisons), and no statistical significance testing is mentioned; these issues do not invalidate the main technical claims but temper the strength of comparative claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The main claims (a transition-based parser TUPA handling reentrancy, discontinuities and non-terminals, using a BiLSTM classifier, and outperforming a range of strong baselines in in- and out-of-domain experiments on the Wiki and 20K Leagues corpora) are supported by experimental results against reasonable baselines and appropriate labeled precision/recall/F measures. The paper’s methodology appears sound (use of strong baselines, BiLSTM feature extractor, dynamic oracle training, and reported hyperparameters), but there are methodological caveats: sentence-level training that discards inter-sentence/linkage/implicit nodes and the conversion of graphs to bilexical graphs/trees for baseline comparison (which the authors acknowledge creates an upper bound) could affect comparability and generality. Overall the technical work is solid and the conclusions are justified, with only minor issues that do not overturn the main claims.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares TUPA to a range of strong, relevant baselines (DAGParser, TurboParser, UPARSE, MaltParser, and a Stack-LSTM parser) using the same UCCA Wiki and 20K Leagues datasets and the same labeled precision/recall/F1 metrics (separately for primary and remote edges). The authors explicitly acknowledge dataset and conversion limitations (sentence-level evaluation, removal of linkage/implicit nodes, and that converting to bilexical graphs/trees for baselines creates an upper bound), and report hyperparameters and thorough related-work discussion. However, it is not fully clear from the summary whether the paper explicitly points out cases where prior methods perform comparably or better or provides detailed per-baseline error analysis, so there are minor omissions in comparative analysis depth. | B(4): The paper compares against multiple appropriate baselines (DAGParser, TurboParser, UPARSE, MaltParser, and Dyer et al.'s Stack LSTM) and evaluates on in-domain and out-of-domain UCCA corpora using labeled precision/recall/F for primary and remote edges. Comparisons appear fair: the same datasets and metrics are used, and the authors explicitly note the conversion to bilexical graphs for baseline comparison (which removes non-terminals and creates an upper bound), as well as other dataset preprocessing limitations. However, the summary does not state whether the paper acknowledges cases where prior methods perform comparably or better, so there is a minor omission in explicitly reporting or discussing such cases.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares TUPA to a range of strong, relevant baselines (DAGParser, TurboParser, UPARSE, MaltParser, and a Stack-LSTM parser) using the same UCCA Wiki and 20K Leagues datasets and the same labeled precision/recall/F1 metrics (separately for primary and remote edges). The authors explicitly acknowledge dataset and conversion limitations (sentence-level evaluation, removal of linkage/implicit nodes, and that converting to bilexical graphs/trees for baselines creates an upper bound), and report hyperparameters and thorough related-work discussion. However, it is not fully clear from the summary whether the paper explicitly points out cases where prior methods perform comparably or better or provides detailed per-baseline error analysis, so there are minor omissions in comparative analysis depth.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against multiple appropriate baselines (DAGParser, TurboParser, UPARSE, MaltParser, and Dyer et al.'s Stack LSTM) and evaluates on in-domain and out-of-domain UCCA corpora using labeled precision/recall/F for primary and remote edges. Comparisons appear fair: the same datasets and metrics are used, and the authors explicitly note the conversion to bilexical graphs for baseline comparison (which removes non-terminals and creates an upper bound), as well as other dataset preprocessing limitations. However, the summary does not state whether the paper acknowledges cases where prior methods perform comparably or better, so there is a minor omission in explicitly reporting or discussing such cases.

main_claims: We present TUPA, the first parser for UCCA.
TUPA supports multiple parents (reentrancy), discontinuous units, and non-terminal nodes, covering the formal properties of UCCA graphs (DAGs).

Coupling TUPA with a neural network classifier and BiLSTM feature extractor yields accurate prediction of UCCA graphs, outperforming a variety of strong baselines in in-domain and out-of-domain settings.
The transition-based, grammarless parser and its transition set can inform development of parsers for other semantic DAG structures and languages with frequent discontinuities.
_summary.methodology: A transition-based parser (TUPA) using a novel transition set (including NODE and SWAP to handle non-terminals and discontinuities) with neural classifiers; feature extraction includes BiLSTM representations over tokens and dense/sparse features, implemented in DyNet and trained with a dynamic oracle and Adam optimization.
datasets: UCCA Twenty Thousand Leagues Under the Sea English–French parallel corpus (20K Leagues)
baselines: Stack LSTM-based parser of Dyer et al. (referred to as the LSTM Parser)
metrics: Labeled F-score (reported separately for primary edges and remote edges)
limitations: Training and evaluation are sentence-level: inter-sentence edges (≈0.18% of edges) were discarded.
Linkage nodes/edges and implicit nodes were discarded from the datasets.
Conversion to bilexical graphs/trees (for baseline comparison) removes non-terminal nodes, creating an upper bound on achievable scores due to this information loss.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - semantic parsing (UCCA parsing, transition-based parsing)
